FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study cohort; baseline 9-10 years, followed longitudinally

FT_IC2.evidence: B12: Pure measurement paper with psychometric analyses (SMAQ/VGAQ factor structure, invariance testing across demographics). Descriptive statistics only (means, comparisons by sex/race). No SM or smartphone as IV with coefficient predicting external outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: B12: Only SM-vs-SM concordance (factor invariance testing, SMAQ/VGAQ equivalence across groups). No inferential test of SM ↔ non-SM-outcome relationship.

FT_IC4.evidence: No extractable numeric SM ↔ outcome coefficient. Chi-square and p-values reported for factor structure comparisons, but these test measurement invariance, not SM predicting behavioral/health outcomes.

FT_IC5.evidence: B13: Pure measurement/concordance paper. Adapted SMAQ and VGAQ scales, demonstrated psychometric properties across populations. No original SM → outcome analysis. Not eligible under B13 (would require at least one SM ↔ non-SM coefficient).

FT_IC6.evidence: Original empirical measurement study; no evidence of duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Paper fails FT_IC2 and FT_IC5 (measurement-only); EC7 logic does not apply.
notes: Measurement paper describing screen usage questionnaire development and psychometric validation. While longitudinal data are used and demographic comparisons are made, no coefficients for SM/smartphone predicting non-SM outcomes are reported. Fails at FT_IC2 and FT_IC5.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study baseline assessment; 9-10 year-old children (N=2217), recruited from 21 sites across US.

FT_IC2.evidence: B6: Video gaming. Self-reported screen time survey item: 'time specifically spent on video gaming.' Video gaming qualifies as app-category/mobile use. Paper dichotomizes VGs (≥21 hr/wk) vs NVGs (0 hr/wk).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple inferential tests: Regression analysis with fMRI BOLD signal (stop signal task, n-back task) as outcomes. Nonparametric comparisons of BOLD signal. Behavioral performance measures (reaction times) with group comparisons and statistical tests. Descriptive: 'VGs performed better on both fMRI tasks compared with the NVGs' (p<0.05).

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric extractable: Adjusted means [SE] for reaction times (287.3 [9.8] vs 300.1 [9.6] ms, SMD 0.04, P=.018); 2-back D' (2.9 vs 2.2 ms, SMD 0.05, P=.002); BOLD signal comparisons by region with P<.002. fMRI statistics in main text and supplement.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis. Video gaming exposure (IV) predicting cognitive performance (fMRI + behavioral outcomes).

FT_EC7_reason: Gaming is IV (predictor), not DV. Outcome is cognitive performance. EC7 applies only when SM/gaming is DV.
notes: Cross-sectional study examining association between video gaming time and cognitive performance (fMRI + behavioral measures). Clear regression/inferential analysis with extractable statistics. Meets all FT-IC criteria.

FT_IC1.evidence: US-based ABCD Study; 21 research sites, 3000+ young adolescents (11-14 years). Prepandemic baseline and COVID-19 pandemic surveys (May-August 2020).

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time (pandemic-era) as predictor. Reported in feature importance: 'screen time variables' (e.g., 'watching news related to coronavirus', 'playing single-player video games', 'browsing the internet'). Qualifies under smartphone/SM use as predictor variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Machine learning classification models (XGBoost gradient boosting trees) predicting psychological distress outcomes (positive affect, stress, anxiety, depressive symptoms). Models fitted to entire dataset; feature importance via SHAP analysis. Inferential test: classification performance with AUC metrics (mean AUC ≥0.70 for both GBT and LogReg models, Table A1).

FT_IC4.evidence: B8/B9 (ML rule): Only SHAP feature importance values reported (mean absolute SHAP values for top 20 features per outcome model). No traditional regression coefficient (β, OR, RR, t, χ², p-value) anywhere in main text or supplement for individual screen time variables predicting outcomes. SHAP values are feature importance rankings, not comparable coefficients. No sensitivity analysis or supplement reporting traditional regression.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis. Longitudinal machine learning models predicting pandemic mental health outcomes (psychological distress and well-being) from prepandemic and pandemic-era psychosocial factors.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is predictor (IV), not dependent variable. Outcomes are psychological distress/affect. EC7 does not apply (requires SM as DV).
notes: Longitudinal machine learning study predicting pandemic mental health in ABCD adolescents. Screen time variables appear in top 20 feature importance rankings for anxiety and depressive symptoms models. However, only SHAP feature importance values are provided; no traditional regression coefficients (β, OR, p-value) are reported for individual screen time predictors. Fails FT_IC4 under B8/B9 ML rule (permutation/SHAP/Gini importance without traditional regression coefficients = NOT_MET).
